coredata.dc:title: Photoactive materials based on semiconducting nanocarbons – A challenge opening new possibilities for photocatalysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Perathoner, Siglinda
Ampelli, Claudio
Chen, Shiming
Passalacqua, Rosalba
Su, Dangsheng
Centi, Gabriele
coredata.dc:description: This perspective paper introduces the concept that nanocarbons and related materials such as carbon dots are an interesting intrinsic photocatalytic semiconducting material, and not only a modifier of the existing (semiconducting) materials to prepare hybrid materials. The semiconducting properties of the nanocarbons, and the possibility to have the band gap within the visible-light region through defect band engineering, introduction of light heteroatoms and control/manipulation of the curvature or surface functionalization are discussed. These materials are conceptually different from the “classical” semiconducting photocatalysts, because semiconductor domains with tuneable characteristics are embedded in a conductive carbon matrix, with the presence of various functional groups (as CO groups) enhancing charge separation by trapping electrons. These nanocarbons open a range of new possibilities for photocatalysis both for energetic and environmental applications. The use of nanocarbons as quantum dots and photoluminescent materials was also analysed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocarbon
Carbon-type photocatalysts
Semiconducting nanocarbons
Carbon nano-dots
Water splitting
CO2 photoreduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nanocarbons as promoter of the photocatalytic behavior
Controlling bandgap in nanocarbons through defect engineering
A broad class of semiconducting nanocarbons
Semiconducting nanocarbons as a new class of photocatalysts
Nanocarbons as quantum dots and photoluminescent materials
Nanocarbon quantum dots for water splitting
Nanocarbon quantum dots for the reduction of CO2
Precise synthesis of carbon photocatalytic material

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The first Brillouin zone of a nanotube showing how the periodicity around the circumference of a carbon nanotube influences its electrical properties. Adapted from ref. [62].

(a) The twisting of a bond within a periodic cell disrupts lattice symmetry, causing band gap opening. (b) A computationally created semiconducting allotrope of graphene with band gap of 1.2eV resulting from patterned defects. Adapted from ref. [63].
TEM image of a CNTs with indications of topological defects and vacancies. Reproduced with permission from Centi and Perathoner [64].

Schematic model of the modification induced on the surface of graphene sheets by introduction of a quaternary nitrogen and how this modification creates defects (indicated with an arrow) as well as modify the charge density on the carbon atoms of a CC bond making possible the coordination of O2, first step for its reduction.

(a) Mechanism suggested of antenna effect of CDs on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 with visible light. (b) TEM image of a CDs with diameter about 2nm. (c) PL spectra of CDs with average size ranging from 1.5 to 2.5nm. Adapted from ref. [90].
Tentative mechanism suggested for the photocatalytic water splitting on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GCDs). Based on indications of ref. [99].
Mechanism of the two-step/two-electron processes of CDs/C3N4 composites for photocatalytic overall water splitting. Based on indications of ref. [97].
Proposed reaction mechanism for reduction of CO2 to CO on pyridinic N-GCD. Based on indications of ref. [103].
Types of nitrogen species that can be incorporated into graphitic carbon.

head.ce:title: Photoactive materials based on semiconducting nanocarbons – A challenge opening new possibilities for photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept.s MIFT and ChiBioFarAm, Section Industrial Chemistry, University of Messina, INSTM/CASPE and ERIC aisbl, V.le F. StagnoD'Alcontres 31, 98166 Messina, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dept.s MIFT and ChiBioFarAm, Section Industrial Chemistry
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: INSTM/CASPE and ERIC aisbl, V.le F. StagnoD'Alcontres 31

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, Liaoning, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This perspective paper introduces the concept that nanocarbons and related materials such as carbon dots are an interesting intrinsic photocatalytic semiconducting material, and not only a modifier of the existing (semiconducting) materials to prepare hybrid materials. The semiconducting properties of the nanocarbons, and the possibility to have the band gap within the visible-light region through defect band engineering, introduction of light heteroatoms and control/manipulation of the curvature or surface functionalization are discussed. These materials are conceptually different from the “classical” semiconducting photocatalysts, because semiconductor domains with tuneable characteristics are embedded in a conductive carbon matrix, with the presence of various functional groups (as CO groups) enhancing charge separation by trapping electrons. These nanocarbons open a range of new possibilities for photocatalysis both for energetic and environmental applications. The use of nanocarbons as quantum dots and photoluminescent materials was also analysed.

Image, graphical abstract
The new concept of nanocarbons as intrinsic photocatalytic semiconducting material, for photocatalysis both for energetic and environmental applications, is discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: The concept of “solar-driven chemistry and energy” indicates the radically new possibility to develop the future of chemistry and energy, the latter in terms of vectors for energy storage and transport, based on new sustainable and low-carbon processes which minimize the use of fossil fuels. This is one of the hottest areas for energy chemistry [1] and key element of this concept is to enable the direct use of renewable energy to drive the processes. Photocatalysis is clearly a critical element for this scope and in fact, the interest on this area has been rapidly growing recently. For example, the papers using as keyword “photocatalysis” increased of about one order of magnitude in the last ten years. Few selected examples of reviews on photocatalysis published this year are reported in refs. [2–9]. In spite of the large interest, the range of photoactive materials used for photocatalytic applications is limited, with TiO2 being still one of the most used. The need to enlarge to visible-light region the photo-activity has significantly stimulated the search of new semiconductor materials [10–16], although stability and performances of these novel visible-light active semiconductors is still often an issue.

Another direction in which active research in photocatalytic materials is oriented, both for environmental and energy-related applications such as water splitting and reduction of CO2, is to improve the photocatalytic properties by preparing hybrid materials, particularly with carbons [17–23]. In most of the cases, however, the carbon component has mainly the function of a modifier to enhance charge collection or transport, or to mediate charge transport, rather than playing a direct role as photo-active component. There is, however, a growing interest on carbon nitride/graphene (g-C3N4/graphene) hybrids [24], nanocarbon-type photosensitizers [25], use of nanocarbon-based photocatalysts [26,27], development of photoresponsive carbon nanomaterials containing azobenzene moieties [28], hybrids between conjugated polymers and carbon nanotubes as photoactive layers in solar cells [29], nanocomposites of porphyrins and nanocarbon materials for photoinduced electron transfer and light energy conversion [30]. We may comment, however, that in spite of the fast growing knowledge on the preparation of these materials, the use of carbon-based photo-active materials is still limited and sometimes not based on a rational design, differently from the use of nanocarbon materials as catalysts or electrocatalysts [31–40]. We will use here the concept of nanocarbons to define the broad area of carbon materials having performances depending on the nano-dimension, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes-CNTs [31].

The aim of this concise perspective paper is to analyse the possibilities offered by nanocarbons as intrinsicphotoactive materials, thus not as modifiers to enhance the properties of another photoactive material. An example is the addition of nanocarbons to semiconductors such as TiO2 to collect better photogenerated electrons and to limit charge recombination [41–47]. We may note that the realization of these hybrid carbon-semiconductor materials leads to more complex phenomena, often not considered or only in part. The interface contact between the semiconductor particles and the nanocarbons often determines the behavior, because the intrinsic properties of titania or other semiconductors result modified from this interaction [17]. There are various additional aspects to consider. The intrinsic properties of the nanocarbon influence the electron-transfer and may introduce radiative paths (carrier trapped centers and electron–hole recombination centers) and/or strain-induced band gap modifications [48]. The nature of the TiO2/nanocarbon interface (which in turn strongly depends also on the specific nature of nanocarbon, particularly the presence of defect sites) determines these effects. Richter and Schmuttenmaer [49] have shown that low mobility in polycrystalline TiO2 nanotubes results from a single sharp resonance arising from exciton-like trap states, with a similar effect expected in TiO2-CNTs nanostructured films. The inverse processes of electron injection into the conduction band of TiO2 can occur [50]. Without a detailed characterization of these aspects, it is not possible to make reliable conclusions whether a carbon-semiconductor hybrid results in enhanced or worse performances [17].

These aspects are scarcely studied, particularly in relation to nanocarbon-semiconductor (TiO2) hybrids, although they are critical for a rational design of these photoactive materials [17,35]. A further relevant mechanism of modification of the photobehavior is related to C-doping of TiO2 nanocrystals [51,52]. Further aspects which may influence (in a positive or negative way) the photocatalytic performances are related to:
•
The enhancement of the light harvesting capability of a semiconductor by dispersion on the nanocarbons; in addition, nanocarbons offer an effective way for an efficient dispersion of the semiconductor preventing from agglomeration, reducing grain boundaries between semiconductor particles and also providing a hierarchical structure for an efficient light harvesting, and eventually for easy access from gas/liquid phase components (in photocatalytic reactions) or an electrolyte (in dye sensitized solar cells).
•
The role of nanocarbons, due to their thermal conductivity, in maintaining more uniform temperature of the semiconductor-nanocarbon hybrid upon irradiation; this is an aspect typically not considered, but which is important.
•
The role of nanocarbon in acting as sinks for electrons, enhancing the lifetime of charge separation. Fermi levels of the nanocarbons are generally below the conduction band of the supported semiconductor nanoparticles. In some cases, depending on the specific characteristics of the nanocarbons, however, the Fermi level may also result above that of the semiconductor; there is an obvious effect of the size and shape of semiconductor nanoparticles, which in turn may be influenced from the interaction with the nanocarbon; also these aspects are not usually considered.
•
Nanocarbons may induce the adsorption of chemicals which may enhance the photocatalytic activity or quench it.
•
Charge creation upon illumination of the semiconductor creates a double layer in solution which affects the transfer rate of molecules across it and in turn influences the reactivity and in some cases also the selectivity (for example, when relatively stable charged species forms, like carbon dioxide anion radical in the photoreduction of CO2); the presence of charge delocalization in hybrid carbon-semiconductor materials influence in principle these aspects, which, however, were not studied in literature.

Therefore, the interaction of semiconductors with nanocarbons induces modification of the intrinsic properties of the semiconductor particles (band gap, charge carrier density, lifetime of charge separation, non-radiative paths, etc.) and of the surface properties, which determine positive or negative changes in the reactivity, but often not rationalized, because the points remarked above are not considered or only in a very limited extend. The consequence is that there are not simple relations between addition of nanocarbons and performances of the hybrid carbon-semiconductor materials. For this reason, often quite contrasting data exist in literature on nanocarbon-semiconductor hybrid materials [17,35].

Although these aspects are important, we will instead focus attention in this perspective paper on another important question, also scarcely investigated: the use of nanocarbons as intrinsic photoactive materials (semiconductor materials, quantum dots or photoactive materials). Some of these aspects are present also in carbon-semiconductor nanohybrids, such as antenna centers to capture the light, systems for charge transport and separation, catalytic centers for the reaction [35,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocarbons as promoter of the photocatalytic behavior
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocarbons may act as a co-catalyst in promoting the photocatalytic behavior. One effect is related to the change in the adsorption modes of the molecules to be converted photocatalytically. For example, the planar adsorption of an alkylaromatic compounds through π-bonds of the aromatic ring gives rise to different products with respect to when a vertical interaction through the alkyl group occurs. Sites near the catalytic centers may modify the chemisorption mode or stabilize some of the reaction intermediates. They have thus a large influence on the catalytic reactivity. Nanocarbons, with the presence of graphitic zones and different modalities of adsorption with respect to oxides or metal particles, offer great possibilities to control and improve the reactivity and selectivity, even this aspect was not extensively examined in literature. In addition, nanocarbon-semiconductor hybrids provide a better (hydrophobic) microenvironment that enhances the local concentration of organic species in aqueous solutions.

Another relevant aspect for the role of nanocarbons as co-catalysts, also not often considered, is the possibility to stabilize reactive oxygen species. CNT characteristics (diameter, chirality, number and type of defects, functionalization, etc.) determine the production of reactive oxygen species during irradiation, and these species may influence the photocatalytic behavior [54]. This could explain why highly defective CNTs show high photocatalytic activity [55]. Several recent studies have been focused on hybrid nanocarbon-semiconductor materials and their use especially in the photocatalytic removal of pollutants both in liquid and gas phase. Eder [50] and Vilatela and Eder [56] have discussed various examples of use of these materials for the photocatalytic treatment of waste streams. The enhancement of photocatalytic properties of TiO2 by nanohybrids with CNTs, fullerene, graphene and other nanocarbons have been discussed by Leary and Westwood [57], while Inagaki [58] has reviewed the promotion of the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 by carbon coating. The application of semiconductor-carbon nanomaterials for the photo-oxidation of pollutants has been discussed in detail by Amal and co-workers [59], while the use of graphene-TiO2 hybrid nanomaterials as photocatalysts for the photo-degradation of pollutants and micro-organisms was analysed by Faria and co-workers [60]. These examples evidence the relevant interest in nanocarbon-semiconductor hybrids both in the area of energy application (water splitting and CO2 photocatalytic reduction) and photocatalytic treatment of pollutants. However, these and other literature studies have typically not considered the possible effects indicated above, for example the co-catalytic role of carbon in changing the adsorption or in generation of reactive oxygen species. In addition, it should be remarked that often the characteristics of the nanocarbon (conductivity, local structure, presence of defects and functional groups, morphology, etc.; as commented before they are critical to determine the properties of the hybrid nanomaterial with the semiconductor) are not considered with the necessary details. Often literature studies put emphasis on the analysis of semiconductor characteristics, while nanocarbons are only described in generic terms, like the use of CNTs, for example, without specifications on their specific characteristics. As a consequence, the precise role by which photocatalytic activity of semiconductor particles can be enhanced by making hybrids with nanocarbons is unclear.

In general, in hybrid carbon-semiconductor materials it is often forgotten to consider that nanocarbons can have intrinsic photoactivity. In fact, carbon nanomaterials may behave as semiconductors, depending on the structure, defects and other characteristics, as discussed in the following sections. This perspective paper will thus discuss these aspects, although it is not aimed to provide a complete state-of-the art. The aim is to evidence that nanocarbons can be another class of photocatalytic materials, not just acting as a modifier of the conventional photoactive materials.

The basic electronic structure of nanocarbons, using graphite as the starting point to analyse the electronic structure, is characterized by hexagonal ordering of carbon atoms, sp 2 hybridization and π-bonding between the carbon atoms. The π-electrons above and below the hexagonal graphene layer are free to move and form an electron band, producing the semi-metal electrical properties of graphite. In carbon nanotubes (CNTs), the graphene sheet can be rolled up forming a tube (single-walled CNT, i.e. SWCNT). By changing the degree of twist in the roll up different helicities or chiralities can be created. The conduction properties of CNTs are strongly dependent on the chirality and the diameter of the tube. For SWCNTs about one third of the possible tube conformations are metallic, whereas the remaining two thirds are semiconducting. The band gap of semiconducting SWCNTs is inversely proportional to the diameter, decreasing from about 0.8eV to 0.4eV on increasing the tube diameter from about 1 to 5nm.

To understand this question, it is necessary to recall the electronic structure of graphene, where the bands are cones, meeting in points at the Fermi surface at the K points in the Brillouin zone. In most directions in the k-space, graphene is a semiconductor, only in special directions (like k y in Fig. 1) graphene is metallic. Fig. 1, reporting the Brillouin zone of a nanotube, shows how the band gap is influenced by the CNT diameter and chirality [61,62]. The pyramidal cones represent the electron energy dispersion of the nanotube surface close to the Fermi energy and the parallel lines show the periodicity around the circumference in reciprocal space. The spacing of the lines is inversely proportional to the CNT diameter and their angle depends on the nanotube chirality. For specific tube diameter and chirality the lines intersect with the apex of the cones and the nanotubes are metallic, i.e. there is an allowed state at the Fermi energy. When the lines intersect elsewhere on the cones, there is a band gap around the Fermi energy level and the nanocarbon shows a semiconducting behavior. For larger diameter CNTs the spacing between the lines is reduced and the maximum possible band gap decreases. The metallic nanotube has two linear bands which cross at the Fermi level. The semiconducting nanotube has two hyperbolic bands, with an energy gap (E g) of about 0.6eV for a 1.5nm diameter.

This band gap is thus too small for useful photocatalytic applications. However, it is possible to modify this band gap and thus create wider band-gap semiconductors, by inducing controlled defects. An interesting theoretical study on this subject was reported by Appelhans et al. [63] who used DFT (Density functional theory) method to investigate two-dimensional carbon semiconductor. Patterned defects can be used to disrupt the sublattice symmetry of graphene so as to open up a band gap. Fig. 2 (a) evidences that small rotations of a bond, periodically repeated through the lattice, collapses the Dirac cones at the graphene K point. Fig. 2(b) shows a computationally created semiconducting allotrope of graphene with band gap of 1.2eV resulting from patterned defects [63]. This band gap is in the range of interest for photocatalytic applications

This defect engineering also allows semiconducting ribbons of C to be fabricated within graphene [63]. Linear arrangements of defects lead to naturally embedded ribbons of the semiconducting material in graphene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controlling bandgap in nanocarbons through defect engineering
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocarbons are always characterized by the presence of defects, even if this aspect is often not considered. Defects are intrinsically related to the presence in nanocarbons of curvatures, mixed sp 2-sp 3 (and sometimes sp 1) bonds and of heteroatoms. We limit the discussion here to CNTs for conciseness, but similar aspects are valid for many nanocarbon materials. In an ideal CNTs structure, the carbon nanotubes do not differ from the arrangement in graphite that also features equivalent atoms. Consequently, one might assume the π-electrons to be delocalized across the whole cylindrical structure. At the end of nanotube, however, the equivalence of bonds is broken. The cylinder is deformed toward the ends and the degree of π-electron delocalization decreases in the entire system. Several resonance structures are possible, including localized double bonds or six-membered rings without aromatic character. Alternating bonds are most frequent near the ends of the tube, while their number decreases toward the center.

Considering the ends of an open SWCNT, the outermost carbon atoms must be saturated by attaching either functional groups or a cap of carbon atoms, obtaining in the latter a closed SWCNT. The shape of caps in closed SWCNT is highly variable. Both approximately hemispheric caps and pointed structures are found, and even concave domains have been observed. Pentagonal defects are possible with a convex curvature and even an inverse curvature (seven-membered rings). These defects lead to beak-shaped caps that exhibit considerably smaller diameters than the tubes themselves. In MWCNT, five-membered rings are also crucial to the formation of the ending cap or open structure of the nanotube, such as the formation of toroidal endings. Defects of individual tubes may influence the MWCNT structure in other ways too. The simultaneous existence of five- and seven-membered rings, for example, makes a bent nanotube. In multiwalled systems, these elbow structures are found in between encapsulated regions. The flexure is thus confined to a few graphene layers.

Defects in MWCNT are always present. It may be briefly differentiated between topological defects which lead to rehybridization (C5 and C7 rings instead of C6 lead to rehybridization between sp 2 and sp 3) and incomplete bonding defects (vacancies, dislocation) (Fig. 3 ) [64]. Functionalization or doping with light hetero-elements may add further modifications with respect to the ideal ordered structure, but doping with light heteroatoms is also creating the sites which allow anchoring supported metals or metal oxides, or to functionalize the CNTs with organic groups. Bernasconi et al. [65] showed with ab initio calculations that a carbon clathrate (hexagonal C40) is suitable to be n doped by Li insertion and p doped by substitutional boron. This material represents an example of n- and p-type tetrahedral carbon semiconductor, alternative to the n-doped diamond-like films. Milne [66] showed that a highly sp 3 bonded (about 85%) amorphous carbon film is a p-type semiconductor, while n-type behavior can be obtained by doping with nitrogen. The optical bandgap reaches a maximum value of about 2.3–2.5eV, which is of interest for photocatalytic applications. Maiti et al. [67] showed that semiconductor grade carbon can be obtained from rice husk. The presence of silica in the starting materials results in the formation of silicon-doped carbon. The further doping with phosphorous and boron produce n- or p-type semiconductor materials. Also sp 2-diamond and cubic-graphite behave as semiconductors, with a direct band gap of 1.66eV and of 2.89eV, respectively [68].

These results evidence that controlled doping is another possibility, in addition to defect engineering, to create and tune the semiconducting performances of nanocarbons. Doping by introduction of heteroatoms (N, O, mainly; during the synthesis or as the result of a post-treatment) is a well-used practice to tailor the electronic structure in these carbon materials and induce specific reactivity and surface properties [69–71]. For example, N atoms can be inserted in three common bonding configurations: pyrrolic, pyridinic, and quaternary N [72] (Scheme 1 ).

Pyridinic N atoms are located at the edges of graphene planes, and each N atom is bonded to two C atoms and donates one π-electron to the π system. In the case of pyrrolic N atoms, they are incorporated into the heterocyclic rings and each N atom is bonded to two C atoms, contributing in two π-electrons to the π system. Graphitic (or quaternary) N refers to the N atoms that replace the carbon atoms in the graphene plane. Such doping N atoms can change the local density state around the Fermi level of N-doped graphitic carbons. Fig. 4 shows a possible mechanism by which doping with N of a graphene layer will induce a modification of charge on the nearlying CC bond making them susceptible for activation of O2 molecules. This is a first step to reduce oxygen and an important element for the activity of metal-free nanocarbons in the oxygen reduction reaction – ORR [72–76]. There is large current interest to go beyond ORR in the use of carbon-based metal-free electrocatalysts, for example for the splitting of water to produce hydrogen [34,77].

Fig. 4 evidences also how the doping with N creates defects in the graphene sheet. Chai et al. [78] showed that a particular structure of a nitrogen pair doped Stone−Wales defect provides a good active site for ORR. The ORR activity of this structure can be further tuned by the curvature around the active site. There are different ways, only partially explored, to modify the surface electronic structure and localized charge of CC bonds in nanocarbons:
(1)
Introduce controlled surface topologies and nearlying heteroatoms (N, B, P, S) in specific positions (as exemplified above for N).
(2)
Introduce curvature associated to nanostructure or topologically-induced carbon defective sites (i.e. by covering a graphene surface with specific roughness).
(3)
Create specific topological/structural defects (pentagons or heptagons within the sp 2 carbon lattice) or bond rotations (when a CC bond is rotated by 90° a Thrower–Stone–Wales defect is created).
(4)
Create hybrid bond structures (i.e. sp 2-sp 3 hybrids like in nanodiamond).

In addition, the introduction of specific functionalities in the carbon lattice (including organic donor–acceptor molecules) near the “active” CC bonds is a further approach for tuning the electronic surface structure in nanocarbons. What is interesting here, is that this mechanism is also a way to create semiconducting localized states in nanocarbons, a concept which was never investigated in relation to possible photocatalytic applications.

Some authors reported the possible co-catalytic effect of carbon in semiconductor-carbon hybrids. For example, Haro et al. [79], studying the photoelectrochemical behavior of carbon/titania composites to understand the mechanisms of the photoinduced processes in these hybrid photocatalysts, indicated different possible mechanisms: (i) the carbon matrix acts as a charge-trapping network that modifies the fate of the photogenerated charge carriers (avoiding recombination and thus enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency); (ii) illumination of the carbon additive renders photogenerated carriers due to π–π* transitions, capable of participating in charge transfer reactions with electron donors present in the reaction medium. On the contrary, we suggest here that the explanation is associated to the creation of localized semiconductor states on the nanocarbon, and thus that the co-catalytic effect of carbon derives from its intrinsic action as photocatalyst.

Fig. 4 suggests also that these “photoactive” carbon sites can be able to activate oxygen, which in the presence of the photogenerated electrons, may generate radical-type oxygen species which could modify the mechanism of photodegradation of pollutants. The photocatalytic activity of semiconductor particles, like of TiO2, derivestypically from the generation of hydroxyl radicals by interaction of OH− with photogenerated holes (h+). Velasco et al. [80] attributed the ability of ash-free activated carbons to interact with UV light to promote the photo-oxidation of phenol to the same reaction mechanism of formation of radical species. This implies, although not explicitly indicated by Velasco et al. [80], the capability of these ash-free activated carbons to act as a semiconductor. As commented above, however, semiconductor carbons have the possibility to generate additional oxygen active species, different from the hydroxyl radicals, which form only under specific conditions on semiconductors. It is thus expected a difference in the reaction mechanisms of organic species degradation between semiconductors like TiO2 and carbon-type semiconductors. This would reflect, for example, in a change in the type of reaction intermediates, in the effect of pH on the reactivity, in the effect of inhibitors of radicals, etc. There are thus various possible ways to study in a more detail this question, which clearly can open new perspectives in the application of photocatalytic methodologies for pollutant removal.

To note that light interaction with nanocarbons may itself induce the creation of semiconducting states. Under electron irradiation it is possible to generate carbon vacancies leading to the formation of extended defect domains (with the presence of pentagonal and heptagonal, and even four-membered carbon rings) showing often semiconductor character. This is likely the mechanism of formation of semiconductor properties in quantum-dot carbon nanoparticles or graphene nanoribbons, which plays an important role in determining the use in the design of advanced photocatalytic materials. Ito et al. [81] showed that the defect creation is related to multi-hole localization, in which bonds are weakened more strongly and more energy is available upon recombination with trapped electrons in comparison with excitons. The multi-hole localized state is considered to be realized by the creation of multiple excitations or multiple excitons. Particularly in highly strained C–C bonds such as in C60 films defects may be induced by low-intensity photoirradiation.

We could also note that charge-carrier mobility in a semiconducting carbon is higher than in conventional types of photoactive semiconductors such as titania. Duerkop et al. [82] cited the high-mobility semiconducting properties of carbon nanotubes, which can exceed 100,000cm2 V−1 s−1 at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A broad class of semiconducting nanocarbons
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth to mention, even if not discussed in a more detail here for conciseness, that also a series of materialsclose-related to nanocarbonsshow photoactive semiconducting properties. Uddin et al. [83], for example, reported that the layer structured hexagonal boron nitride carbon semiconductor alloys, h-(BN)C, offers unique abilities of bandgap engineering (from 0 for graphite to ∼6.4eV for h-BN) and electron conduction control (from semi-metal for graphite to insulator for undoped h-BN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Semiconducting nanocarbons as a new class of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Previous sections have shown that there are quite interesting developments in the area of nanocarbons (or related type of materials) as semiconductor materials and how to tune these properties by manipulation of curvature and defect engineering, the latter related mainly to a controlled doping with light heteroatoms. The discussion has also remarked that there are some analogies between semiconductor nanocarbons and conventional materials such as TiO2. However, there are also intrinsic differences, which are the elements of interest opening new possibilities in designing photoactive materials. One of these relevant differences is the presence of localized semiconductor states, which characteristics may be tuned by the mechanisms indicated above with thus the possibility, in principle, to create a multiple band-gap material. This opens also a range of new possibilities in creating synergies with conventional semiconductors (in nanohybrid materials) [17].

Although semiconductor nanocarbons have been investigated mainly in the area of materials for electronics or as modifiers of conventional semiconductors (as discussed in the introduction), we have shown here that the concept of semiconducting nanocarbons as a new class of photocatalysts opens novel and breakthrough ideas in the design of advanced photocatalysis both for energy applications (water splitting, CO2 photoreduction) and pollution removal or water/air sterilization. On the other hand, the success in this direction derives from the ability to understand better the processes of creation/tuning semiconducting properties in nanocarbons (nanocarbon engineering [84]), and how these properties are related to the photocatalytic activity. Clearly, a challenging problem in the design and fabrication of carbon photocatalytic materials is the ability to realize a precise control of doping, including the doping degree, doping sites, which needs a precise chemical method, like click chemistry, to resolve this problem. So these aspects will be discussed later.

It is worth to remember that carbon nanodots properties (that will be discussed in the following section) are closely related to the semiconducting properties of nanocarbons. Zhang and co-workers [85] remarked that carbon dots (CDs) have emerged recently as a promising new class of metal-free photocatalysts, displaying semiconductor-like photoelectron properties and showing excellent performance in a wide range of photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic applications. CDs are relatively ease to synthetize, show a unique structure and an adjustable composition, may be further modified by surface functionalization, show an outstanding electron-transfer efficiency and have a tuneable light-harvesting range (from deep UV to the near-IR). Carbon nanostructures show also photoluminescence properties, which are related to photocatalytic properties. Kozak et al. [86] remarked that photoluminescent nanosized allotropes of carbon have attracted considerable interest, because of their diverse optical properties which depend on their crystal structure, size, morphology and chemical functionalization. Different mechanisms by which carbon nanostructures exhibit photoluminescence (PL) are the following: i) excitonic PL in carbon nanotubes, ii) thermally activated delayed fluorescence in spherical fullerenes, iii) the formation of impurity–vacancy colorcenters in nanodiamonds, iv) the presence of aromatic sp 2 domains in reduced graphene oxide, and v) the generation of surface chromophores or defect-related PL in carbon dots. Some of these aspects are in relation to the mechanisms of creation of localized semiconducting states in nanocarbons, as discussed above. However, a difference is present when localized states or more extended bands are generated. There are thus analogies, but also differences, which should be better understood to develop a more generalized approach in using nanocarbons as novel photocatalytic materials. Accordingly, Cayuela et al. [87] remarked the distinction between semiconductor and carbon-based dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon quantum dots and carbon nanodots).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocarbons as quantum dots and photoluminescent materials
ce:section.ce:para: The term carbon dots (CDs) indicate nanosized carbon materials with typically dimensions below about 10nm and composed by sp 2/sp 3 hybridized carbon atoms and often heteroatoms which induce specific fluorescence properties. Different types of CDs have been reported: i) graphene quantum dots, consisting of one or a few layers of graphene and thus having a 2D character, and ii) carbon nanodots, which may be considered quasi-0D nanostructures. There are thus different quantum confinement effects in the two classes of CDs. This is a quite active research area, after the initial discovery by Xu et al. [88]. Although CDs can be prepared with different relatively inexpensive ways, the production of homogeneous, reproducible CDs with defined properties is still an issue. CDs find increasing use, even if mainly at laboratory scale, in photocatalytic solar-to-energy conversion materials, due to their low cost, excellent performance and being based on not toxic materials [89]. Li et al. [90] first introduced a one-step alkali metal-assisted electrochemical method for the deposition of CDs (having size-dependent photoluminescence) on semiconductor particles (TiO2), in order to promote the photocatalytic behavior by antenna effect. This method allows to produce CDs exhibiting stable and strong photoluminescence (quantum yield about 12 %) with a homogeneous distribution over the TiO2 particles.

Fig. 5 reports the suggested scheme of antenna effect of CDs in promoting visible-light activity of TiO2, an example of TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy) image of a CD, and how the photoluminescence (PL) spectra change as a function of the size of CDs. Li et al. [90] reported that small CDs give UV light emission (about 350nm), medium-sized CDs (1.5–3nm) give visible light emission (400–700nm) and large CDs (3.8nm) give near-infrared emission (about 800nm). Therefore, by controlling the size it is possible to realize a broad emission range of light.

An issue not fully discussed by Li et al. [90] and often not analysed in other papers, is how to use this light absorption by CDs to transfer the energy and enable charge separation in the semiconductor, if the latter has a band-gap at energies higher than the light absorbed by CDs. For example, CDs absorb in the 400-600nm range, while absorption band for TiO2 falls at around 300–320nm (adsorption edge). Li et al. [90] indicate that the CD absorbs visible light, and then emit shorter wavelength light, but this "upconversion" is possible only by a multiphoton adsorption, which is not a probable effect in CDs. In plasmonic photocatalysts, the coupled oscillations of electrons and electromagnetic fields (localized surface plasmons) give rise to the formation of high-energy resonant states upon (visible-light) adsorption, which decay may occurs with generation of energetic charge carriers [91], but this mechanism cannot be translated to the CD case. A critical question to understand is thus the behavior of CDs as antennas in promoting the photocatalytic activity of large band gap semiconductors such as TiO2. On the other hand, the possibility that these CDs acts directly as active photocatalysts, as suggested in the previous section, was never considered in analysing the data. This possibility could explain the observed behavior.

The recent review by Yu et al. [85] indicates a possible mechanisms to explain this effect of CDs in promoting the visible-light photocatalytic activity of wide band-gap semiconductors (TiO2 and various others such as Fe2O3, ZnO, Ag3PO4, or Cu2O) [92,93]: i) the upconversion effect (i.e. the absorption of lower energy wavelengths by CDs and re-emission of higher energy photons, able to excite the electrons from the valence to the conduction band in the semiconductor, and ii) the effect of CDs in promoting charge separation and increasing the number of charge carriers available for photoreactions. The latter effect is analogous to that present in heterojunctions between the semiconductor and metal nanoparticles, having suitable bands below the Fermi level. However, this mechanism is not likely in CDs, neither their capacity to store charges. On the contrary, the co-catalysis activity of CDs, due to the presence of localized semiconductor states, may be a reasonable interpretation. This is consistent, on the other hand, with the many literature data (see Yu et al. [85]) reporting the photocatalytic behavior of CD-based photocatalysts, particularly in the degradation of dyes. This is well consistent with what discussed in the previous section and the comments on the capacity by semiconducting nanocarbons to photogenerate active (radical-type) oxygen species.

There is abundant literature on the interfacial effects between two semiconductors and the promotion effect of CDs on the photocatalytic performances of semiconducting materials such as TiO2, Fe2O3. The behavior of CDs as intrinsic photocatalysts, rather than as antennas, is a way to rationalize these effects from a different perspective. Doping with heteroatoms such as N is an effective way to increase the band gap in nanocarbons, as commented in the previous section, giving the opportunity to shift band gap to an energy range more interesting for photocatalytic applications. In agreement, doping with N was observed to be an effective mechanism to promote the performances of CDs [94]. As an example, Ma et al. [95] prepared N-doped CDs by ultrasonic reaction between glucose and ammonium hydroxide, and observed higher photocatalytic behavior with respect to undoped samples.

It should be commented that other functionalities present in CDs, such as carbonyl groups, may be also involved in the mechanisms of promotion of the photocatalytic behavior. One possibility is that they act as trapping centers for electrons and thus promote charge separation. Hu et al. [96] showed that the presence of CO and COOH groups in CDs enhances the photocatalytic behavior. These authors indicated that the intramolecular hydrogen bonds formed among the oxygen-containing groups on the CDs increases the band-bending, facilitating the separation and stabilization of the photocarriers. This effect could be in addition to the role of these oxygen functional groups, particularly carbonyls, in acting as electron trapping centers, which we believe is the dominant effect. Also in this case, the absence of detailed studies has to be remarked.

The addition of CDs to various types of semiconductors (from TiO2 and WO3 to CdSe and SrTiO3) [85] was reported to enhance the photo- or photoelectro-catalytic generation of H2. In some cases, CDs in conjunction with others nanocarbon-type materials (g-C3N4) [97] or even alone [98] were reported to be active in the photoproduction of H2 from water. Yeh et al. [99] showed that N-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (N-GCDs) could act as visible-light photocatalysts for overall water splitting. H2 and O2 were observed in a stoichiometric ratio, confirming the water splitting activity with visible light. The performances were about half of those observed by one of the most active system (Rh2−
y Cr y O3/GaN:ZnO). The mechanism proposed was based on the hypothesis that p- and n-type semiconducting domains are present in N-GCDs, due to presence of both undoped and doped areas. Fig. 6 summarizes the mechanism proposed:
(i)
The bandgap of the N-GCDs is about 2.2eV, which is suitable for visible-light exciton generation;
(ii)
the p- and n-type domains are sp 2 areas acting as Ohmic spaces and allowing to have an efficient p–n junctions for electron–hole separation;
(iii)
H2 and O2 are produced at the edges of the n- and p-type domains, by reaction of protons and water with electrons and holes, respectively.

The suggested mechanism for water splitting over N-GCDs is largely hypothetical, being not proved the effective presence of these n- and p-type domains and their structure. Nevertheless, the mechanism indicated by Yeh et al. [99] is well consistent with the point remarked in the previous section that semiconductor-type nanocarbons can be characterized by local semiconducting domains in a conductive matrix. From this perspective, n- and p-type domains can coexist in nanocarbons, although the exact nature of these domains should be better understood. It is evident, however, that a number of interesting possibilities, in terms of design of advances photocatalysts, derives from the possibility to extend this concept of nanosized n- and p-type domains.

It should be remarked how these nanocarbon semiconducting materials are conceptually different from the “conventional” semiconductors such as TiO2, because in the same conductive matrix may be present semiconductor domains with tuneable characteristics, sites to enhance local charge separation by trapping electrons (the CO groups previously remarked) and areas which enhance charge transport. It is thus possible, in principle, to realize on nanoscale photoactive materials which could be realized today only on the micron scale. Although there are very challenging issues for the possibility to prepare these materials, the perspectives look thus highly promising.

Band structure and pinning at the interface, formation of heterostructures, charge separation, quantum confinements, and charge carrier mobility are between the many aspects which are expected to be different in semiconducting nanocarbons with respect to conventional semiconductors. In addition, the possibilities to control the local structure in nanocarbons is somewhat higher. In this sense, nanocarbons open a range on new possibilities for photocatalysis both for energetic and environmental applications, although the realization of this potential requires a large conceptual effort. Nevertheless, it should be commented that actual data are already interesting. As as example, Yeh et al. [100] reported a quantum efficiency (QE) of 12.8% at 420nm for Pt-doped N-GCD in the photocatalytic H2 evolution in aqueous triethanolamine. Although in the presence of a sacrificial agent, this is an interesting result, superior to that of g-C3N4 and several Pt-doped conventional photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocarbon quantum dots for water splitting
ce:section.ce:para: Quite interesting synergies can be observed between CDs and other type of carbon-based materials, such as g-C3N4. CDs/C3N4 nanocomposites which have been proposed as metal-free photocatalysts for efficient visible-light overall water splitting [97]. For an optimal CDs loading, a QE of 16% at 420nm, 6.29% at 580nm, and 4.42% at 600nm without any sacrificial agents was reported. The overall solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency reached 2.0%, and the activity was stable over 200 days. This is even more remarkable by considering that the authors indicated a two-step mechanism of oxygen reduction (Fig. 7 ). In the first two-electron transfer step, C3N4 generates H2 and H2O2 under visible-light irradiation. In the next two-electron transfer step, the CDs catalyse the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2. Photogenerated hydrogen peroxide (or more likely peroxide surface species) is strong oxidizing species and thus only their very fast decomposition avoids that they act as oxidant for the carbon material. Stability may be thus an issue, but the good durability of these CDs/C3N4 nanocomposites indicates that this critical aspect can be overcome.

It may be also noted that there are some unclear aspects in this mechanism, such as the hypothesis that two electron oxidation of water generates H2 and H2O2, while the further two electron oxidation of H2O2 generates only O2. There are thus aspects to clarify further, but the concept of the activity of CDs mainly in the decomposition of H2O2 (or better surface peroxide species, those likely existing in photocatodic conditions) suggests a different interpretations of the promotion by CDs of the photocatalytic behavior of TiO2 (and analogous semiconductors). TiO2 shows a very high efficiency in water splitting with UV radiation, but much lower efficiency when modified (through different mechanisms) to induce activity in the visible light region. An interpretation is that TiO2 generates H2O2 (or surface peroxide species) by water oxidation, with the slow step consisting in the further decomposition of hydrogen peroxide to give oxygen. UV radiation is effective in the latter reaction, and so all the process occurs with high rate and thus high quantum efficiency. However, visible light is not able to decompose H2O2 which thus accumulates on the surface quenching the surface processes and decreasing the overall activity. One of the possible actions of CDs in promoting the visible light activity of TiO2 could be to catalyse the further conversion of the surface peroxo species and thus increase the efficiency of the process through this mechanism. In addition, during the process of deposition of CDs, some of the carbon atoms can dope the titania, creating intermediates states which lower the band gap. These considerations thus evidence the need to analyse better the mechanism of promotion of photocatalytic performances by addition of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocarbon quantum dots for the reduction of CO2
ce:section.ce:para: CDs or CDs-modified samples show interesting behaviors in the photocatalytic conversion of CO2. The first results were reported by Sun and co-workers [101] using CDs prepared by nitric acid oxidation of carbon powders, and further modified with oligomeric poly(ethylene glycol) diamine (PEG 1500N). Then Au or Pt nanoparticles were deposited on the surface of CDs by photochemical reduction. CO2 was reduced to formic acid (as the main species) with a quantum yield of about 0.3% under UV irradiation. The production rate of formic acid on Au/CDs reaches a value of 1.2mmolh−1 g−1 at a pressure of CO2 of 104kPa. For comparison, the rate is 0.15mmolh−1 g−1 on Au/TiO2, i.e. about one order of magnitude lower. However, Au/TiO2 produces also other products of CO2 reduction and likely the mechanism of reaction on Au/CDs is likely the photogeneration of H2 (or the equivalent, i.e. e−/H+) which then reacts with CO2. The deposition of CDs on Cu2O nanospheres forms methanol by photoreduction of CO2 with yields of 55.7μmolh−1 g−1 under solar-light irradiation [102].

The reaction mechanism of CO2 reduction with CDs was analysed also theoretically. Yang et al. [103] investigated by DFT (density functional theory) N-GCDs on p-type Si-nanowires. The experimental evidences are that the N-GCD decreases the overpotential and increases the CO selectivity in the electrochemical reduction of CO2. The band gap of N-GCD (about one eV for a size larger than 3nm) is suitable to accept electrons from the conduction band of p-type Si, allowing CO2 reduction on the surface of negatively charged N-GCD. Pyridinic N-doping sites were indicated as the major active sites, due to the lowest binding energy with CO2, in agreement with other literature data [104]. CO and H2O are then generated via a two-step protonation and reduction process. Fig. 8 summarizes the proposed reaction mechanism for the reduction of CO2 to CO on pyridinic N-GCD.

A recent work [105] on the origin of the activity in N-doped nanocarbons in the electrocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide has demonstrated that between the different N species formed by doping nanocarbons (not thus a single type as often assumed in theoretical studies), the most efficient nitrogen chemical state for the reduction of CO2 is quaternary nitrogen, followed by pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen. A very selective (about 90% current efficiency for CO formation) and stable (at least over 60h) behavior in the electrocatalytic reduction of CO2 was observed, with a reaction mechanism slightly different of that indicated in Fig. 8. The reduction of CO2 proceeds through an initial rate-determining transfer of one electron to CO2, which leads to the formation of carbon dioxide radical anion (•CO2 −). The initial reduction barrier is too high on pristine CNTs, resulting in a very high overpotential at which the hydrogen evolution reaction dominates over CO2 reduction. Differently, the nitrogen atoms stabilize the radical anion, thereby lowering the initial reduction barrier and improving the intrinsic activity. These results were reported for the electrochemical reduction of CO2, but likely an analogy in the reaction mechanism is present.

The above results on the photocatalytic activity of N-doped CDs and their behavior in the water splitting indicate that these materials have a great potential for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2, but it is necessary to better control the nature of the N species present and how they change the semiconducting properties of these materials.

Photoluminescence (PL) of CDs [86,87] is a property somewhat related to the photocatalytic activity, because there are common aspects in terms of mechanism of generation of the charge separation. However, PL is the emission of light from electronically excited states that occurs spontaneously after the system absorbs light, while photocatalysis refers to the generation of charge separation after the adsorption of light and the following steps of "catalytic" reaction of the photogenerated e−/h+ to either generate the effective "active" species (for example, hydroxyl radicals in the mechanism of photocatalytic conversion of pollutants) or directly the products of reaction (or their intermediates), such as in the mechanism of water splitting. A difference is that the processes of light emissions in PL occur in a time scale (picoseconds) comparable to charge separation. On the contrary, the "catalytic" reaction rate of the photogenerated e−/h+ is typically much slower (in the millisecond range) and thus a critical problem in photocatalytic applications is to realize mechanisms (for example, bycreating heterojunctions) which allow to bridge this difference in the time scale, and avoid side reactions of these reactive species, including PL.

So, in principle, photoluminescent CDs are not good for photocatalysis, because light is re-emitted at a lower energy and the mechanism of upconversion discussed before is not possible in the absence of multiphoton adsorption or other mechanisms which enable this upconversion. Nevertheless, the photoluminescence and photocatalysis concepts are often mixed and not clearly differentiated in CDs, although often they show both properties. Therefore, it is necessary to understand much better when one path prevail over the other and thus identify which structural characteristics in CDs are the enabling factors to control these paths.

With respect to other semiconducting nanocarbons having very small band gap such as CNTs and thus PL in the near infrared region (NIR), CDs often emit blue or green light. The lifetimes are typically in the picosecond−nanosecond range, but their quantum yields (QYs) vary widely, from the rather inefficient spherical fullerenes to ultrabright CDs with QYs above 80% [106]. In general terms, as for semiconducting properties, the PL is enhanced in CDs by introducing structural disorders, for instance by functionalization to create regions of sp 3-hybridized carbon centers surrounding embedded aromatic domains. This modification gives rise to band gaps, making the material photoluminescent [107]. In addition to these effects, the presence of functional groups (such as CO, COOH) influences the mechanism of PL. The mechanism of PL in nanodiamond (mostly sp 3) is instead different and related to impurity−vacancy pairs [108]. In general terms, five are the mechanisms by which nanocarbons can exhibit PL [86]. Some of these mechanisms appear to be specific to particular nanostructure classes such as CNTs (excitonic PL) [109], spherical fullerenes (thermally activated delayed fluorescence) [110] and nanodiamonds (PL arising from impurity−vacancy centers) [108]. These mechanisms, however, are not related to the photocatalytic behavior, differently to those related to semiconducting behavior (specific domains) and to surface groups/defects. These latter mechanisms are those present in CDs, although the exact origins of PL and how to control the PL behavior are still largely not understood. The most characteristic properties of PL in CDs is the possibility that different color emissions can be obtained from s single solution of CDs using appropriate excitation wavelengths or bandpass filters. In part this effect is related to CDs size and thus the spatial confinement of the luminescent electron–hole pair. However, other aspects are also relevant, as can be argued from the discussion in the other sections. Experimental results evidence size-dependent emission colors in different fractions of crystalline CDs, but also that different fractions with similar particle sizes may have very different emission colors [86]. One of the points, as remarked regarding semiconducting properties, is that it is necessary to analyse the domains present in CDs and to correlate the size of these domains to PL properties. Fuyuno et al. [111] showed that larger GCDs had a higher probability of containing larger sp 2 clusters and thus emitting at lower energies (due to reduced size confinement). Theoretical studies on the variation of energy gaps and UV−vis absorption spectra with the sizes of aromatic domains support the concept of conjugated system size dependent PL in CDs. Zhao et al. [112] showed by DFT calculations that single-layered sp 2 carbon structure has a fluorescence (S1→ S0) with wavelengths redshifted as the size of the dots increased, from 669nm in a model consisting of 132 C atoms to 755nm in a model with 170 C atoms. This indicates that in principle such a type of sp 2 ordered domains should be minimized to enhance the photocatalytic properties. On the other hand, radiative recombinations in carbon nanomaterials can derive also from trapping (defect-type) sites and it is thus evident how the specific nanostructure and defects are a critical factor to determine charge separation versus PL or non-radiative paths. PL may originate also from functional groups present in CDs, like oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, epoxy) [113]. All these facts indicate that the PL from CDs should not be attributed to the particle as a whole, but to localized units within it, as previously discussed for semiconducting properties. In addition, the surface chemistry of CDs is another relevant parameter to consider. It is thus a quite complex phenomenon and for this reason still only understood in very general terms. However, this is the key to develop better PL materials and to determine how to optimize their photocatalytic properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precise synthesis of carbon photocatalytic material
ce:section.ce:para: The use of nanocarbons as advanced photocatalytic materials requires a precise synthesis of these catalysts with the necessary control in terms of type and location of light heteroatoms dopants, defects, nanostructures to create the semiconducting properties we discussed in the previous section. This design and fabrication of carbonic photocatalytic material is certainly a very challenging problem, which requires to develop new ideas and approaches to control the doping degree and the modalities to create the doped sites and/or defects. In addition, surface chemical functionalization of the nanocarbons is an excellent method to further tune the properties [114].

A number of reviews have discussed the synthesis of nanocarbons and how to create supramolecular assemblies with nanocarbons. Bhattacharya and Samanta [115] reviewed the possibilities to create controlled gel-​nanocomposites assembled from different metal-​based nanoparticles or nanocarbons (fullerene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphenes). Segawa et al. [116] discussed the selective and predictable synthesis of structurally uniform CNTs with single chirality through the organic synthesis of CNT segments and the organic template assisted growth of CNTs. He et al. [117] analysed how to control the morphologies, structures, dispersion, and interface of nanocarbon (CNTs and graphene) composites prepared by in situ chemical vapour deposition (CVD). Bottari et al. [118] investigated the preparation of hybrid nanomaterials based on phthalocyanine-​nanocarbon ensembles. Shearer and Eder [119] reviewed the synthesis strategies of nanocarbon hybrids. Klappenberger et al. [120] discussed the opportunities for the fabrication of distinct carbon-based scaffolds incorporating units with both sp 2- and sp 1-hybridizations, such as carbyne chains, or two-dimensional (2D) graphyne or graphdiyne networks, by linking terminal alkynes. Wang et al. [121] analysed the different strategies developed to prepare one-dimensional (1D) nanomaterials such as nanowires (NWs), and nanotubes (NTs), with or without supported Pt metal nanoparticles, in relation to their use as electrocatalysts in fuel cells. Yang et al. [122] reviewed the controllable synthesis of fluorescent CDs. Liu et al. [123] discussed the controlled synthesis of aligned CNTs, in particular for electronic applications and with reference on how to obtain specific nanotube orientation, dimension and diameter, and chirality. Yu and Lee [124] analysed how to control carriers in CNTs, for example how to convert them from a p- to an n-type behavior. Various different approaches were discussed: i) impurity doping that employs conventional and interstitial insertion of group III or V materials, ii) chemical doping that induces charge transfer between chemicals and CNTs, iii) carrier control that utilizes the work function difference between metal and CNTs, iv) electrostatic doping that controls the carrier type using a gate bias, and v) ambipolarity that does not use chemical doping. This not-exhaustive short summary of few selected reviews of the synthesis of nanocarbons evidences the growing knowledge on the synthesis of nanocarbons. Nevertheless, several of the relevant points remarked before to obtain controlled (photo) response in carbon nanomaterials remain a challenge.

For example, the specific papers on how it is possible to realize a controlled introduction of defects in nanocarbons, CNTs for example, are extremely limited. Yoshida and Takeda [125] showed by high-resolution ETEM (Environmental transmission electron microscopy) observations that the deformation of nanoparticle catalysts (NPCs) during the growth of CNTs triggers the formation of various defects in CNTs. The small deformation of NPCs at the interface with CNTs gives rise to the formation of bends and disorder of the interlayer spacing in CNTs. The changes in the diameter and number of graphitic layers in CNTs are caused by the large protrusion on and shrink deformations of NPCs. This study provides insightful strategies to control the creation and grown of defects in CNTs. Jadi and Setiadi [126] showed theoretically that a longitudinal Stone Wales (SW) defect is more stable than a circumference SW defect. Barrier energy is the parameter to control the SW defect in CNTs. In the case of circumference SW defect, armchair edge (5,5) CNT become semiconductor with the band gap of 0.0544eV. Wilson et al. [127] have shown that a carboxylic acid-functionalized diazonium reagent can be used to introduce a defined number of sp 3 defects into electrically contacted CNTs. Although defect engineering in CNTs is sometimes claimed [128–130], this is still a challenge, particularly in relation to use this method to induce controlled semiconducting properties in nanocarbons.

There is thus the need to intensify research on realizing precise synthesis methods for carbon photocatalytic materials, along the requirement discussed in the previous section which can be a guide to identify better the specific targets to obtain in terms of carbon properties.

Nanocarbons represent a new type of photoactive materials conceptually radically different from the conventional oxide-type semiconductors like TiO2 and thus offer unique new possibilities to design next-generation materials needed to address the challenging area of “solar-driven chemistry and energy” as defined in the introduction. However, this potential requires two conditions:
(1)
to identify the basic mechanisms by which these materials can be photoactive and what are the differences from those for conventionaloxide-type semiconductors; a direct consequence is also to reinterpret on these bases some of the results already available in literature and to develop the conceptual framework for a rational design of nanocarbon-type photoactive materials;
(2)
to use the knowledge in point 1 to develop specific preparation methodologies for precise control of the nanocarbon structure and doping/defect engineering or surface tailored functionalization which leads to prepare the aimed materials.

It is evident that the second stage should in principle be consequent to the first, or at least operate in a synergetic parallel way. We may note, however, that activity on the preparation of nanocarbons is significantly larger than that in the first area, although not centered on the rational synthesis of the nanocarbons with the characteristics necessary for being photoactive in the largest majority. For this reason, this perspective review was focused principally on the first part, although the second is clearly very challenging. It is necessary, however, to turn the perspective in the synthesis of nanomaterials to identify how to i) dope with heteroatoms in specific locations, ii) realize an effective defect engineering, iii) use the many possibilities offered by surface functionalization to control the effective characteristics of nanocarbons. In parallel, theoretical approaches should explore better this concept of localized semiconducting zones, with n- or p-character, and how this is in relation to above aspects.

In addition to these general aspects, we have evidenced in this paper both a series of areas in which it is necessary to focus the attention, or that may be interpreted from different perspectives, because largely the concept of nanocarbons as intrinsic photoactive materials is not present in literature. There are thus many aspects which need to be explored or results to be reinterpreted in a different way. We may just recall here some of them which are discussed in a more detail in the previous sections.

In hybrid nanocarbons-semiconductors materials a clear recommendations is to put more attention, and to develop related characterization methods, to understand i) the specific characteristics of nanocarbons (nanostructure, defects, presence and localization of doping elements), ii) the nature of the nanocarbon-semiconductor interface, and iii) the relative synergy between nanocarbons and semiconductor characteristics. There are several additional aspects to be understood better. Between them, may be cited, for example, a) the role of nanocarbons in heat dispersion, b) in acting as heterojunction with creation of local distorted sites at the nanocarbon-semiconductor interface, c) in the modification of the adsorption properties and of double layer, and d) in influencing charge mobility (short and long-range). A critical element, however, is that the nanocarbons (including CDs) may act directly as co-catalyst in photobehavior. Another relevant question regards the generation of reactive oxygen species.

In nanocarbons, a general issue related to their surface reactivity, including the photoreactivity after the creation of charge separation, is a better understanding of the different ways, only partially explored, to modify the surface electronic structure and localized charge of C–C bonds in nanocarbons. We have indicated four possibilities: i) introduce controlled surface topologies and nearlying light heteroatoms in specific positions, ii) introduce curvature associated to nanostructure or topologically-induced carbon defective sites, iii) create specific topological/structural defects or bond rotations, and iv) create hybrid bond structures. Part of these mechanisms are associated also to the generation of localized semiconducting states, and thus a synergic effect between creation of photoactive pathways and creation of new types of active sites exists.
To remark that light interaction with nanocarbons may itself induce the creation of semiconducting states and thus in-situ characterization methods during photocatalysis are necessary.

For CDs, we have remarked how often they do not act as antenna, for the difficulty in realizing upconversion processes (as often interpreted today), but they act directly as broad-band photocatalysts. There is the need to understand better the processes of creation/tuning semiconducting properties in CDs (nanocarbon engineering), and how these properties are related to the photocatalytic activity. PLs properties of CDs are important aspects, but without a proper understanding of how to pass from PL to photocatalytic properties, the great potential of these materials remains essentially phenomenological.

The aim of this perspective paper is to introduce the concept that nanocarbons and related materials such as carbon dots (CDs) are interesting intrinsic photocatalytic semiconducting materials, which use is not restricted to addition as modifiers of the existing (semiconducting) materials, as reported in most of the literature. Often relevant improvements in the photocatalytic properties have been obtained and there are multiple effects by which nanocarbons can induce an enhanced photocatalytic behavior. Some of them were discussed especially in the introduction. However, the direct role of nanocarbons as effective photocatalyst, particularly to improve the behavior with visible-light radiation, is the likely most relevant aspect determining the performances, although typically not recognized.

For this reason, we have analysed here the semiconducting properties of the nanocarbons, typically considered for electronic applications rather than for photocatalytic ones. For the latter, band gap within the visible-light region is necessary and we have shown here, how defect band engineering and introduction of heteroatoms in nanocarbons are two enabling elements to achieve this scope. Controlling/manipulating curvature is another aspect, although less investigated.

It was also remarked how these materials are conceptually different from the “classical” semiconducting photocatalysts, even with some analogies. In semiconducting nanocarbons, semiconductor domains with tuneable characteristics, are embedded in the conductive carbon matrix, and various functional groups, which may enhance charge separation (as CO groups) by trapping electrons, can be also present. Band structure and pinning at the interface, formation of heterostructures, charge separation, quantum confinements, and charge carrier mobility are between the many aspects which are different in semiconducting nanocarbons with respect to the conventional semiconductors. In addition, the possibilities to control the nanostructure in nanocarbons is somewhat higher. In this sense, nanocarbons open a range of new possibilities for photocatalysis both for energetic and environmental applications. The realization of this potential requires, however, a large conceptual effort and the development of precise synthetic methods. We have often remarked here how understanding on these systems is still too low, but the potential great. These systems, however, are quite complex, with synergetic or mutual inhibition effects. It should be avoided thus to limit discussion to only few specific aspects, like that the addition of nanocarbons to a semiconductor improves electron collection. Addressing this complexity is a challenge, but offers the possibility to develop conceptually new materials.

The use of nanocarbons as quantum dots and photoluminescent materials was also analysed, because both are quite fast growing novel scientific areas. There are common aspects between PL carbon materials (CDs) and semiconducting nanocarbons, but differences which should be considered. Between these the fact that PL properties should be minimized to realize an effective charge separation and minimize the non-radiative paths. The better integration of all these aspects in a common understanding of the use of nanocarbons as intrinsic photocatalysts is necessary, beginning from the better identification of the exact nature of nanocarbon elements: coherent and defective domains, presence of heteroatoms and their localization, curvature effects and modifications in the strain of CC bonds, presence of different carbon coordinations (sp 1-sp 2-sp 3) in domains and at interface/heterojunctions between these domains.

We have also presented some examples about the photocatalytic behavior of especially CDs for both environmental applications and energetic ones (water splitting and CO2 conversion), although the aim of this review was not a state-of-the-art report, but instead an effort to stimulate readers to consider from a different perspective the possibility to use nanocarbons as intrinsic photoactive catalysts. These carbon materials open new possibilities, notwithstanding the highly challenging need of a better understanding of their structural characteristics and relation to the complex problem of the nature of the efficient charge separation creation (especially with visible light) and reduction of the competitive pathways of recombination (radiative and non-radiative).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Italian MIUR gh the PRIN Project 2015K7FZLH SMARTNESS "Solar driven chemistry: new materials for photo- and electro-catalysis" is gratefully acknowledged. S.C. also thanks SINCHEM, a Joint Doctorate programme selected under the Erasmus Mundus Action 1 Programme (FPA 2013-0037).

Nanoporous Materials for Energy and the Environment
